headlines.title: Thousands expected for Mexico-South Korea World Cup watch party in San Jose
headlines.description: San Pedro Square in downtown San Jose is once again expected to be packed with thousands of soccer fans during Thursday evening’s watch party for the World Cup group stage match between Mexico and South Korea. To manage the anticipated capacity crowds at San Pedro Square, the city is implementing strict security measures and expansive overflow protocols. Metal barriers have been installed at all entrances to San Pedro Square to prevent vehicle access, and municipal officials promised an increased presence of both police officers and private security guards. “We’ll have San Pedro Street with our video screens there,” said Tommy O’Hare, San Jose’s sports and special events director. “Then we’ll open up Sharks Way, which is between San Pedro Street and Almaden. On St. John’s, we’ll have an additional screen there. So we have some overflow screen area there as well.” O’Hare added that if the crowds continue to swell, the city has contingency plans to erect additional viewing screens within walking distance of the main square. San Pedro Square got a taste of just how passionate Mexican soccer fans are last Thursday during the World Cup opener between Mexico and South Africa. “As far as official numbers, let’s just say there wasn’t a square inch that was uncovered,” San Jose Sports Authority Executive Director John Poach said. “It was well over 12,000 people here in the plaza. We’re expecting even more because this is an even bigger game.” Kickoff for the Mexico-South Korea match from Guadalajara Stadium in Mexico is scheduled for 6 p.m. Both countries enter the match with three points in Group A play. Mexico defeated South Africa 2-0 in its opener, while South Korea topped Czechia 2-1 in its first match. San Jose 12 hours ago San Jose 16 hours ago San Jose plans massive World Cup viewing party as VTA breaks ridership records

headlines.title: California billionaire tax proposal is slated to qualify for the November ballot
headlines.description: A proposal to temporarily increase taxes on billionaires in California to counter federal cuts to healthcare for low-income people has sufficient public support to qualify for the November ballot, the state’s top elections official said. Secretary of State Shirley Weber, a Democrat, said Wednesday night that petitioners have collected more than the roughly 875,000 signatures needed to place the proposed tax before voters. It will qualify June 25 unless proponents pull the measure. The proposal, backed by the Service Employees International Union Healthcare Workers West, would impose a one-time, 5% tax on individuals whose net worth exceeds $1 billion and who were living in the state as of Jan. 1, 2026. The goal is to generate $100 billion in revenue, mainly to fund the state’s Medicaid system with some money going to food assistance and education programs. States have been debating how to respond to the major tax breaks and spending cuts legislation President Donald Trump signed last year. The proposal has already divided Democrats and major labor unions and triggered an expensive campaign to defeat it. The proposed tax is backed by prominent progressives including Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders. The measure has faced staunch pushback from Silicon Valley tech moguls as well as Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom and prominent players in Sacramento. They include the California Medical Association and California School Boards Association, which helped launch a committee this week to oppose it. Newsom also opposed a ballot measure in 2022 to increase taxes on the wealthy, which would have funded programs that help people buy electric cars or install more chargers. Voters rejected it. Critics say the measure would decrease state revenue over time by pushing the ultrawealthy to leave, taking the money they would contribute in income taxes with them. That would deal a huge blow to a state that relies on its top 1% of earners for nearly half of its personal income tax revenue. The nonpartisan Legislative Analyst’s Office estimates that the proposal would generate tens of billions of dollars in the first few years, but that income tax revenues could subsequently decline by hundreds of millions of dollars annually. Since the proposal was announced in October, Google co-founder Sergey Brin has donated $82 million to a political committee called “Building a Better California” that backs a variety of initiatives designed to blunt the billionaire tax proposal. It has raised more than $118 million, counting Brin’s contributions, from fewer than a dozen donors. State lawmakers passed budget bills this week that aim to raise revenue in other ways, including by extending a tax on healthcare providers. Newsom and legislative leaders agree to this approach, Senate President pro Tempore Monique Limón said. “The budget, as approved by the Legislature and now being negotiated with the Governor, does not include the billionaire’s tax,” the Democrat said in a statement. “Instead, it reflects additional revenues to address our long-term structural deficit.”

headlines.title: Golden Gate Bridge tolls set to increase 50 cents
headlines.description: All Golden Gate Bridge toll rates will go up 50 cents on July 1, bridge officials said Wednesday. The increases include: FasTrak Account rate: to $10.25, from $9.75 Pay-As-You-Go rate including license plate accounts and one-time payments/short-term license plate account: to $10.50, from $10 Toll Invoice rate: to $11.25, from $10.75 Carpool rate: to $8.25, from $7.75 The increase is part of a multi-year program approved by the Golden Gate Bridge board of directors.

headlines.title: SFPD says license plate readers ‘improperly' accessed by out-of-state, federal agencies
headlines.description: San Francisco police say they have disabled access to its network of Flock Automated License Plate Reader cameras after it was “improperly queried” for out-of-state and federal agencies. Police said the issue was discovered through regular audits last month. “The audit did not find any inquiries referencing immigration enforcement or reproductive rights investigations, and the federal agencies identified do not include Immigration and Customs Enforcement or the Department of Homeland Security,” police said Wednesday night in a statement. Automatic license plate reader technology provides a tracker system that helps multiple law enforcement agencies identify vehicles associated with criminal suspects, witnesses and victims, as well as point out drug trafficking, stolen vehicles and Amber Alerts. California law places clear restrictions on sharing license plate reader information with out-of-state or federal agencies. San Francisco police said their data was accessed by the Northern California Regional Intelligence Center and the Western States Information Network. “NCRIC and WSIN’s access to SFPD ALPR network currently remains disabled,” police said. San Francisco Jun 10 Oakland Apr 28 The intelligence center’s website says it is part of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security’s information sharing strategy and serves as the regional “all major crimes” intelligence fusion center for the federal Northern District of California. Some Bay Area cities ousted license plate reader camera manufacturer Flock Safety after privacy watchdogs raised concerns about federal agencies being able to access the camera data and using it for targeted surveillance. All the queries to SFPD’s network were simultaneously made to between 531 and 763 other law enforcement agencies, police said. “There were 299 improper inquiries over approximately one year, which account for 0.005% of inquiries over that period,” police said. The department said it previously authorized license-plate data sharing with NCRIC as allowed by state law. No out-of-state or federal agencies ever had direct access to SFPD’s Flock system, it said. “Upon discovering the improper searches, Chief Derrick Lew immediately turned off NCRIC’s access to SFPD’s ALPR system and initiated an internal review,” police said.

headlines.title: San Jose officer lawfully killed heavily armed man who shot at police, DA says
headlines.description: A San Jose police officer acted lawfully in fatally shooting a heavily armed man dressed in military fatigues after he fired at officers, prosecutors said Wednesday. Police were called to Karim Khan’s home on July 6 “after his parents said that their mentally ill, 37-year-old son was being violent with them,” the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office said. The man was armed with a pump action shotgun, a bolt action rifle, a revolver and a semi-automatic pistol, and disregarded attempts by police to get him to surrender, according to prosecutors. “His conduct that afternoon placed the officers in an impossible situation that required lethal force in self-defense,” prosecutors said. Footage from a body-worn camera showed Khan and Officer Sebastian Wisniewski firing almost simultaneously, according to prosecutors. The district attorney’s office investigates fatal encounters with law enforcement to determine whether lethal force was legal. Under law, officers can use deadly force when they or others face imminent danger, prosecutors said.

headlines.title: San Francisco leaders to vote on new Embarcadero park plan
headlines.description: San Francisco leaders on Thursday will take another major step in reshaping the waterfront. Now that Vaillancourt Fountain is gone, city leaders hope to move forward with a plan to transform Embarcadero Plaza into a new waterfront park. Leaders will look to lock in the design for what replaces the fountain. San Francisco Recreation and Parks is already working with outside partners on potential plans and is set to vote on those plans Thursday morning. The latest renderings show the area where the fountain once stood will be filled in and transformed into an open area. Leaders are considering a $40 million plan to transform the site into a 5-acre destination park that would combine the formerly-named Justin Herman Plaza and Sue Bierman Park and connect Market Street to the Ferry Building’s attractions. Supervisor Danny Sauter recently introduced legislation accepting up to $20 million in private funding that would still need to be approved by the board of supervisors. Voter-approved funds from a 2024 bond will partially fund the project. San Francisco May 6 San Francisco Apr 27 Work begins to dismantle San Francisco's Vaillancourt Fountain

headlines.title: World Cup brings record ridership and long lines for VTA in Santa Clara
headlines.description: The World Cup has brought tens of thousands of soccer fans to the temporarily named San Francisco Bay Area Stadium in Santa Clara so far, topping local transit ridership records and resulting in some wait times for passengers. So far, two World Cup matches have been played at the stadium, with four more matches coming up. Wednesday, Santa Clara Valley Transportation Authority announced that 39,600 passengers rode VTA on Tuesday night for the Austria-Jordan match, a new ridership record. The previous ridership record – 37,000 passengers – was set during Saturday’s match between Qatar and Switzerland. VTA noted these numbers were higher than the ridership for the Super Bowl in February and the Taylor Swift Eras Tour shows. “Consistently increasing over the last few events we’ve had at Levi’s because people are realizing that it’s an efficient, affordable way to get to the stadium and avoid traffic and high parking prices,” VTA said in a statement. But some fans attending the World Cup matches noted the high ridership seemed to translate to longer lines getting to transit. In particular, fans noted long lines to take VTA after matches, saying that they had to wait for multiple trains before finding one with enough space to take them back. Transportation Jun 9 VTA Jun 5 VTA ready to transport thousands of fans during World Cup

headlines.title: Skeptical Bay Area motorists watch pump prices closely after U.S.-Iran deal
headlines.description: President Donald Trump announced Thursday night that a preliminary agreement has been reached between the United States and Iran, aiming to halt a months-long war and potentially easing pressure on global energy markets. Trump made the announcement upon his arrival at the Palace of Versailles on the final night of the G7 summit, telling reporters the deal had been signed. According to a senior U.S. official, the preliminary 14-point agreement would lift the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports and reopen the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz. In return, Iran would reaffirm its commitment not to develop or procure nuclear weapons. The diplomatic breakthrough is being watched closely by drivers in the San Francisco Bay Area, where high fuel costs continue to strain household budgets. Energy experts suggest that if the peace terms hold, relief could be immediate. “Absolutely next week is possible,” said Robert Chapman Wood, an energy expert and professor at San Jose State University, referring to a potential drop in prices at the pump. Wood added that if the framework progresses, it would be in Iran’s financial interest to export oil as rapidly as possible, which would also make it politically difficult for future U.S. policy shifts to dismantle the agreement. However, major questions remain regarding how quickly volatile energy markets will react and whether both nations will adhere to the terms. The preliminary deal has already drawn sharp criticism from political opponents who argue the administration is conceding too much leverage. The framework requires the U.S. to coordinate with Gulf nations to establish a $300 billion reconstruction fund for Iran while allowing the country to resume international oil sales. “It does smack of the kind of appeasement that our administration rejected in the Obama-Iran nuclear deal,” said former Vice President Mike Pence. When pressed by reporters about the multibillion-dollar reconstruction fund, Trump insisted the U.S. would not be funding it directly. “Only if they’re doing things right,” Trump said, adding, “we’re not putting up money.” Administration officials stated that a finalized agreement is expected to address broader security concerns, including the Iranian regime’s funding of terrorist organizations and its remaining ballistic missile program. Meanwhile, Bay Area motorists expressed deep skepticism that the preliminary diplomatic breakthrough will translate to lower everyday expenses. “I’m sorry but the president keeps saying it’s ended, it’s ended,” said Lorri Brinkerhoff, a motorist from Benicia, California. “But then I keep reading if it doesn’t end we’re going to bomb them.” Other local drivers noted that standard fill-ups currently cost up to $120, with some reporting costs approaching $100 even before their vehicles run completely out of fuel. Vice President J.D. Vance is scheduled to travel to Switzerland on Friday to sign the official memorandum. Under the terms of the framework, both diplomatic teams will then have a 60-day window to negotiate and solidify a final treaty.

headlines.title: Thousands pack downtown San Jose for Colombia vs. Uzbekistan World Cup match
headlines.description: Thousands of Colombian fans packed San Pedro Square for the World Cup match between Uzbekistan and Colombia Wednesday, and even more are expected for Thursday’s match between Mexico and South Korea. World Cup fever was on full display in San Jose’s San Pedro Square. Colombian fans erupted in cheers after Colombia scored its first goal against Uzbekistan. “It’s my first time here in San Pedro Square, I love the energy, I love how all the community gets together to celebrate, and we can really enjoy safely,” said Camila, who was supporting Colombia. Security was tight as fans celebrated. Most people were supporting the South America country, but there were some also cheering on Uzbekistan. “It’s our first time in the World Cup, and I’m extremely happy,” Anastasia said. Even bigger crowds are expected Thursday night for the Mexico-South Korea watch party. San Pedro Square got a taste of just how passionate Mexican soccer fans are last Thursday. “As far as official numbers, let’s just say there wasn’t a square inch that wasn’t covered, well over 12,000 here in the plaza, so we’re expecting even more because this is an even bigger game,” San Jose Sports Authority Executive Director John Poach said. The city says there will be an increased police presence and more private security at Thursday’s event. There are also contingency plans to manage the overflow crowds, and make sure everyone can get a glimpse of the game. “We have San Pedro Square, and we have the next street to it open up. We’re also planning to open up Sharks Way and put up more screens there and have another additional overflow-screen area as well. And if the crowd gets even bigger, there are also plans to put additional screens walking distance from San Pedro Square,” 2026 San Jose Sports and Special Events Director Tommy O’Hare said. Restaurants and bars are also gearing up for all the fans. “Really stocking up, blew through much more than we anticipated, making sure that our staff is kept fresh, rotating them in, we’re fortunate that we have other stores that we can pull from,” Sushi Confidential VP of Operations Douglas Cookerly said. “Last week that Mexico was here, we didn’t really think that the crowd was going to be that much, so this week we’re well prepared, we’re staffing up, we’re prepping up the bar and kitchen,” Must Be Thai & Bar Owner Thalia Swangchaeng said. Game starts at 6 p.m.

headlines.title: San Francisco's historic Club Deluxe to reopen Thursday
headlines.description: A storied San Francisco jazz club that fell victim to the pandemic and a landlord dispute more than three years ago is set to reopen Thursday night under new ownership, bringing live music back to the heart of the Haight-Ashbury neighborhood. The Deluxe—originally known as Club Deluxe—has undergone a two-year restoration process. Its new owners hope the revived space will tap into San Francisco’s ongoing post-pandemic rebound and serve as a crucial hub for live entertainment and creativity. Christian Beaulieu and co-owner Jay Bordeleau are taking over a space that has been a neighborhood fixture since 1928. Over the last 98 years, the building has served as a creamery, a tailor shop, a gay disco, and a swingin’ jazz club. During the restoration process, workers uncovered various artifacts inside the walls highlighting the building’s past lives. Despite the extensive overhaul, the new owners recognize that the venue’s identity belongs to the community. “I feel it belongs more to the regulars and to the neighborhood than it does to me,” Bordeleau said. “I’m just allowed to be the steward of the space right now.” The reopening comes at a transitional time for the city’s music scene. While the owners look forward to welcoming long-term patrons, they are also aiming to attract a new generation of artists and listeners. According to Beaulieu, a new wave of jazz musicians is breathing life into the local scene by blending traditional sounds with modern genres. “A lot of people are coming to play the room for the first time,” Beaulieu said. “And then there’s also a cross-pollination of electronic music and jazz happening that’s very popular.” The owners are also eyeing San Francisco’s current artificial intelligence technology boom. They hope some of the wealth generated by the tech industry will find its way into the local arts scene, preserving the city’s cultural heritage. “The city is beautiful because people before us patronized the arts,” Bordeleau said, emphasizing that digital conveniences cannot replace the visceral experience of a live performance. “People want that in-life, real, tangible… you can’t DoorDash a band.”

headlines.title: San Jose plans massive World Cup viewing party as VTA breaks ridership records
headlines.description: World Cup fever is mounting in the Bay Area as local transit systems break ridership records and cities prepare for massive influxes of international soccer fans. San Jose officials announced plans to host the city’s largest World Cup viewing party yet at San Pedro Square, gearing up to welcome more than 25,000 fans for the upcoming group stage match between Mexico and South Korea on Thursday. The announcement follows a packed afternoon at the square, where large crowds gathered to watch England face off against Croatia. “It’s the World Cup, who wouldn’t wanna to be here to see people from all over the world and other cultures and watching great soccer?” said San Jose resident Chad Fegley. To manage the anticipated capacity crowds, the city is implementing strict security measures and expansive overflow protocols. Metal barriers have been installed at all entrances to San Pedro Square to prevent vehicle access, and municipal officials promised an increased presence of both police officers and private security guards. “We have San Pedro Square, and we have the street next to it open up,” said Tommy O’Hare, San Jose’s sports and special events director. “We’re also planning to open up Sharks Way and put up more screens there and have another additional overflow screen area as well.” O’Hare added that if the crowds continue to swell, the city has contingency plans to erect additional viewing screens within walking distance of the main square. The surge in local soccer enthusiasm is already testing regional infrastructure. More than 39,000 fans traveled by the Santa Clara Valley Transportation Authority (VTA) light rail system to attend the Jordan-Austria match at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium in Santa Clara. The single-day total set an all-time ridership record for VTA, eclipsing the agency’s previous record set during the Super Bowl. “We think it’s because we had a lot of visitors from other countries who ride trains more readily than they do here,” said VTA spokesperson Stacey Hendler Ross. While VTA successfully met its operational goal of clearing the stadium crowds in under two hours after the game ended, the transit agency is adjusting its strategy for upcoming matches based on early logistical hurdles. “On Saturday we learned that it’s not just enough to have ambassadors out there telling people where to go,” Ross said. “We also need more signs, so we put up big, tall signs that tell people go this way to Milpitas and that way to Mountain View.” Transit officials are also warning travelers that service will not run indefinitely. VTA plans to explicitly notify passengers that trains will cease operations three hours after matches conclude, advising fans not to rely on the light rail system late into the night.

headlines.title: Bay Area sales help set new record for California median home prices
headlines.description: Median home prices in California are at an all-time high with Bay Area sales helping set a new record for the state, the California Association of Realtors said. New numbers released by CAR show the median price for a single-family home in the state is $930,260 as of May 2026. The record is a 2.3% jump since April 2026, which is more than double the historical average increase between the two months. In the Bay Area, the region also set a record high last month with a median price for a single-family home at $1.45 million — a 3.6% jump from April. Three Bay Area counties saw median home prices over $2 million, the highest across the state. These include San Francisco, San Mateo and Santa Clara counties. San Francisco County’s median is currently $2.2 million, a 22.2% increase since May 2025. San Mateo County’s median is currently $2.4 million, a 9.1% increase since May 2025. Santa Clara County’s median is currently $2.1 million, a 2.5% decrease since May 2025. Not every Bay Area county saw an increase: Marin County saw 4% drop and Santa Clara County experienced a 3.3% dip. See CAR’s county breakdown of median home prices and the month-to-month change below.
